Compensation is not competitive for the level of output and sacrifice required — especially compared to industry roles.

Promotions and raises are slow and based on timing and optics, not performance or value added.

Mental, emotional, and physical burnout is common, and support is superficial at best.

title: Working at PwC helped me build technical skills quickly. The name carries weight on a resume, and I appreciated the formal training early on.
pros: Working at PwC helped me build technical skills quickly. The name carries weight on a resume, and I appreciated the formal training early on.
cons:
As an Audit Associate at PwC, I experienced one of the most grueling work environments of my career. The busy season is relentless — you’re expected to completely sacrifice your personal life for nearly four months out of the year. Late nights until 2–3 AM are not uncommon, and weekends are often treated as regular workdays.

What made the experience worse was the lack of support from senior team members and management. Rather than fostering a collaborative environment, seniors and managers frequently overloaded associates with excessive tasks while providing minimal guidance. When help was offered, it often came with condescending attitudes, dismissive remarks, or outright belittling. There was little to no regard for employee well-being.

Despite putting in long hours and significant effort, I was laid off after two years with little explanation or appreciation for the sacrifices I made. Overall, my time at PwC left me burned out and disillusioned. I would not recommend this role to anyone who values work-life balance, a supportive team, or long-term career growth.

title: Main pros are the exit opportunities and consistent and clear promo schedule (assuming the firm is doing well - people stopped getting promoted when business slowed down). Flexibility with WFH + travel. Exposure to huge household name clients which is good for resume.
pros: Main pros are the exit opportunities and consistent and clear promo schedule (assuming the firm is doing well - people stopped getting promoted when business slowed down). Flexibility with WFH + travel. Exposure to huge household name clients which is good for resume.
cons:
Management sees associates as disposable. We lost 75% of our associates to quitting during my short <2 year tenure. Management encourages competition between associates (was directly told that I should be competing with other associates). One of the senior managers I worked with had some serious anger issues and took it out on associates - he apologized for this once but still pretty uncomfortable otherwise. Managers assume you know everything and some get frustrated when you say you don't know how to do something even though you've never been trained on it before. You also get paid less than your peers who went straight to doing finance at tech companies. You also get less benefits and perks. Although the exit ops are generally good (multiple recruiters were reaching out to me and that's how I got my new job), you can get pigeon-holed into valuations / accounting type jobs if you stay too long. Sometimes you work crazy long hours (18 hour days) and sometimes it feels like the work is meaningless. Sometimes the opposite happens and there is no work to do and you only have 2 hours of work per day -> this leads to your utilization score going below the threshold which is bad for your bonus / promotions. Sometimes budgets are super tight on projects and you get in trouble for actually billing all the hours you work. Tons of monotonous boring admin work. Associate role will likely be automated by AI and become obsolete. Huge push to use 'acceleration centers' (India and Argentina employees) more and more to cut costs. There's a bunch of other issues I can list but you get the idea.

Moving to a new job has shown me what it's like to work somewhere where management actually cares about younger employees.

pros:
PwC is a place that really comes down to the teams you’re staffed on. The teams that are staffed properly could really show you how to do the work, give you a realistic time table for it, and help you grow your experience in that area of tax.

There were plenty of opportunities to move to other teams as well and to look for work outside of your immediate group.
cons:
Towards the third year of my career I noticed much of our work being given to the AC teams (offshore outsourcing) to the degree that it felt like you had to scramble for additional work if it was not busy season.

This led to further issues once busy season did hit, as the lack of exposure to relevant tasks left you ill-equipped to tackle some of the more difficult tasks during close or reporting periods.

title: - In office policy is pretty relaxed, most people are in 2-3 days per week on average, in busy season it tends to go up and in less busy times people go in much less. - Firm benefits are amazing, healthcare plans and 401k match are good, time off is accrued but carries over YoY and paid out upon leaving if you have balance left. - Large start class if you begin out of college is great, there are not many companies where you can start your career with hundreds of other kids in your same office in some cases so you get to meet a ton of people and build your network early. - Learning and development is a huge part of our job, constant opportunities to grow your knowledge base in both your specific field as well as branch out to other topics. - The firm pays for CPA study materials from Becker along with one of each test. There also is a CPA bonus which is amazing. - Opportunities to get involved in your community outside of work with different groups and organizations and we get paid time off to do community service which is wonderful. - A lot of firm sponsored happy hours and fun events are put on for your specific groups which allows you to meet co workers and partners in a relaxed atmosphere. - The holiday party and promotion party are amazing, the firm goes all out and puts on amazing events. The pay scale is extremely transparent which allows you to know exactly what you will be paid at the next levels as well as bonus percentages. Bonuses are based on performance and you are placed into three different buckets to receive a percent of your salary.
pros:
- In office policy is pretty relaxed, most people are in 2-3 days per week on average, in busy season it tends to go up and in less busy times people go in much less.
- Firm benefits are amazing, healthcare plans and 401k match are good, time off is accrued but carries over YoY and paid out upon leaving if you have balance left.
- Large start class if you begin out of college is great, there are not many companies where you can start your career with hundreds of other kids in your same office in some cases so you get to meet a ton of people and build your network early.
- Learning and development is a huge part of our job, constant opportunities to grow your knowledge base in both your specific field as well as branch out to other topics.
- The firm pays for CPA study materials from Becker along with one of each test. There also is a CPA bonus which is amazing.
- Opportunities to get involved in your community outside of work with different groups and organizations and we get paid time off to do community service which is wonderful.
- A lot of firm sponsored happy hours and fun events are put on for your specific groups which allows you to meet co workers and partners in a relaxed atmosphere.
- The holiday party and promotion party are amazing, the firm goes all out and puts on amazing events.
The pay scale is extremely transparent which allows you to know exactly what you will be paid at the next levels as well as bonus percentages. Bonuses are based on performance and you are placed into three different buckets to receive a percent of your salary.
cons:
- Busy season hours aren't great, it seems like we are slightly understaffed and it begins to show during our busiest months.
- Some individuals are promoted to the next level a bit early, not allowing them to develop fully which in turn has negative impacts on team productivity and work product. 
- Similar to every firm, you will run into some managers and higher ups who micro-manage. There is no way to review higher ups, only to review people lower than you in the management structure so there is nobody giving feedback to these people over their management styles.
